turns.content: I'm building a new library and I've heard you should use #![deny(unsafe_code)] at the crate root, but what's the actual difference between putting that directive at the top level versus relying on default lint checks somewhere in the middle of my project?

The key distinction is that #[deny(unsafe_code)] is a global attribute placed directly above your module or lib.rs to enforce safety rules across the entire compilation unit. When you add it there, the compiler will halt immediately if any unsafe blocks, unsafe functions, unsafe impl blocks, or unsafe extern blocks appear anywhere within your crate's code, including in proc-macro crates that expand into this library. Without this directive, Rust only warns about unsafe code using the 'unsafe_code' lint level set to 'warn', allowing builds to succeed even with dangerous code present. This means relying on default checks alone might let you ship a crate with hidden vulnerabilities from extern blocks or unsafe implementations that bypass explicit warnings,
That makes sense, so does this mean I can never use unsafe in my own code if I want the deny flag to work?

Not at all; you are not forbidden from using unsafe constructs in your own code. The directive simply enforces that every instance of unsafe blocks, unsafe functions, or unsafe implementations must be explicitly allowed by a #[allow(unsafe_code)] attribute placed immediately above them. This pattern ensures developers know exactly where safety boundaries exist rather than hiding them inside complex control flow. You can still use unsafe in your own code as long as you document it and justify the necessity,
Okay, so if I have a dependency that uses unsafe internally, will #![deny(unsafe_code)] stop my build?

Yes, placing #[deny(unsafe_code)] at the crate root will cause your build to fail if any of your dependencies contain unsafe code. This is by design because you likely want to audit and optionally replace dangerous dependencies with safer alternatives before releasing your public crate. If a dependency like serde_json or tokio uses unsafe internally for performance, placing this directive there forces you to either update that dependency version or refactor its usage in your project,
I see, so what if I just want to audit my code without breaking the build?

You can temporarily set the lint level to 'warn' instead of 'deny' at the crate root to allow builds while still catching unsafe code. Once you have audited your code and ensured no unnecessary unsafe blocks exist, you can then switch it back to deny to enforce safety guarantees for all future contributors. This gives you flexibility during development while maintaining strict enforcement once the project is stable.
One more thing, I noticed some crates on crates.io show a green 'Safe' badge, does that mean they definitely don't use unsafe?

The Safe badges on crates.io indicate that the maintainer has verified their code contains no unsafe blocks or functions and explicitly sets #[deny(unsafe_code)] at the crate root. However, this verification is voluntary and not guaranteed by Rust itself; a crate might claim to be safe but still contain hidden unsafe code if the author made a mistake or changed the code later without updating the badge status. Always verify safety claims yourself by reading the source code,
Got it, so my goal is to make my library completely free of any unsafe constructs?

Exactly, aiming for zero-unsafe crates is a strong practice that improves trust and security. You should systematically search your entire codebase for any remaining unsafe blocks or functions, then either remove them if they are not necessary or add #[allow(unsafe_code)] with clear documentation explaining why they remain. This ensures your library remains safe by default and avoids potential vulnerabilities from hidden unsafe constructs.
I'm confused why we have to put #![deny(unsafe_code)] at the crate root. What exactly does it catch, and when should I use unsafe?

The `#![deny(unsafe_code)]` attribute is like a red light for safety in Rust — if you ever call `unsafe`, even inside a function or impl block, the compiler will yell loudly. It's a safeguard that encourages safe coding by making unsafe code stand out. Imagine you're building a house and someone tells you 'no nails allowed.' You'd be careful everywhere to avoid using nails, right? That’s what this lint does for your crate: it makes sure no unsafe bits slip in.
But if I have some unsafe code inside a proc-macro or lib module, will that trigger the deny?

Yes, it does. Even inside macros or libraries, `#![deny(unsafe_code)]` applies globally to your crate. If you use unsafe in those parts, the compiler won't let you compile until fixed. It’s a strict policy that keeps the whole crate safe, not just some parts.
So if I want to write zero-unsafe crates, what's the best approach?

For zero-unsafe crates, always start with `#![deny(unsafe_code)]` and enforce it. Use only safe APIs, avoid raw pointers or unsafe blocks unless absolutely necessary (and even then, document carefully). Consider using abstractions to hide unsafe details from users — for example, wrapping unsafe code behind safe interfaces. This way, you ensure your crate remains safe without sacrificing performance or features.
Should I also audit dependencies that have unsafe code?

Good idea! Always check crates.io badges for 'unsafe' indicators. Some crates may have warnings or even unsafe flags, so be cautious with those. For your own code, keep `#![deny(unsafe_code)]` active and fix any unsafe bits you find during compilation. This dual approach — linting your crate and auditing dependencies — will help maintain overall safety.

Hi, I'm working on a Rust crate for my personal project. The thing is, I want to enforce that no unsafe code sneaks into the library. But I'm not sure what exactly #![deny(unsafe_code)] will catch and how it should be used.

Great question! Placing `#![deny(unsafe_code)]` at the crate root is a great way to enforce safety guarantees. It tells the compiler that your codebase shouldn't contain any unsafe blocks (`unsafe {}`), unsafe functions, unsafe implementations of traits, or unsafe foreign function interface (FFI) declarations (`extern`). This helps ensure that your library remains safe and predictable for users.
So, if I put it in Cargo.toml as a feature or just inside my main.rs file? And what happens if someone tries to use unsafe code?

`#![deny(unsafe_code)]` must be placed at the crate root level, not inside your source files. If you try to compile with unsafe code, the compiler will throw a hard error and refuse to build your crate. This is very helpful for catching mistakes early. Imagine if someone tried to use `unsafe {}` in one of your functions; the compiler would yell at them immediately.
What about proc-macros? Can they have unsafe code?

Good question. Proc-macros are a bit trickier because their compilation is separate from your crate's main build. But if you're writing a procedural macro, it's still better to avoid unsafe code inside the macro itself. If you must use `unsafe`, make sure that the macro only exposes safe APIs and doesn't expose unsafe blocks or FFI calls. Otherwise, users of your macros might accidentally write unsafe code without realizing it.
Okay, so if I want my crate to be zero-unsafe, what are some best practices?

Good strategy! You should avoid all unsafe code in your library. That means no `unsafe {}` blocks or FFI calls. Instead, design your API around safe abstractions. If you ever find yourself tempted to use `unsafe`, step back and consider if there's a safer alternative. This approach is especially important for crates that others will depend on, as it ensures better reliability.
What about auditing unsafe code in dependencies? Sometimes I can't control what third-party crates do.

That's right. You can't always guarantee that all dependencies are safe. To mitigate this, consider using `cargo audit` to check for known vulnerabilities and unsafe patterns in your dependencies. Additionally, you can use tools like `cargo deny` or `cargo-fuzz` to fuzz test your crate and catch unsafe usages even if they come from deps.
I see. And how do I know if a crate is safe when browsing crates.io?

Good point! Crates.io has safety badges now, which indicate whether a crate uses `#![deny(unsafe_code)]`. This badge can be found on the crate page under 'Safety'. It's like a quick sanity check before you integrate a crate into your project. If the badge shows 'safe', it means the maintainer is consciously avoiding unsafe code.
So, to summarize: I should put `#![deny(unsafe_code)]` at the root of my crate, avoid unsafe code inside everything I own, and be mindful of unsafe patterns in dependencies. Right?
Exactly! That's a solid approach. It'll help you build safer crates from day one and reduce runtime surprises for your users.
